Multivariable Logistic Regression Analysis of Diabetes and Modifiable Risk Factors for Heart Disease: 2022 BRFSS Data

Author

Claire Xu

Week 1: Data Import and Outcome Recoding

library(tidyverse)

# Load the data 
heart <- read_csv("/Users/clairexu/Desktop/Heart Disease Project/csv files/2022/heart_2022_no_nans.csv")

# First look
head(heart)
# A tibble: 6 × 40
  State  Sex   GeneralHealth PhysicalHealthDays MentalHealthDays LastCheckupTime
  <chr>  <chr> <chr>                      <dbl>            <dbl> <chr>          
1 Alaba… Fema… Very good                      4                0 Within past ye…
2 Alaba… Male  Very good                      0                0 Within past ye…
3 Alaba… Male  Very good                      0                0 Within past ye…
4 Alaba… Fema… Fair                           5                0 Within past ye…
5 Alaba… Fema… Good                           3               15 Within past ye…
6 Alaba… Male  Good                           0                0 Within past ye…
# ℹ 34 more variables: PhysicalActivities <chr>, SleepHours <dbl>,
#   RemovedTeeth <chr>, HadHeartAttack <chr>, HadAngina <chr>, HadStroke <chr>,
#   HadAsthma <chr>, HadSkinCancer <chr>, HadCOPD <chr>,
#   HadDepressiveDisorder <chr>, HadKidneyDisease <chr>, HadArthritis <chr>,
#   HadDiabetes <chr>, DeafOrHardOfHearing <chr>,
#   BlindOrVisionDifficulty <chr>, DifficultyConcentrating <chr>,
#   DifficultyWalking <chr>, DifficultyDressingBathing <chr>, …
str(heart)
spc_tbl_ [246,022 × 40] (S3: spec_tbl_df/tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)

 $ PhysicalActivities       : chr [1:246022] "Yes" "Yes" "No" "Yes" ...

 $ DifficultyErrands        : chr [1:246022] "No" "No" "No" "No" ...

 $ ChestScan                : chr [1:246022] "No" "No" "Yes" "No" ...

 $ AgeCategory              : chr [1:246022] "Age 65 to 69" "Age 70 to 74" "Age 75 to 79" "Age 80 or older" ...

 $ WeightInKilograms        : num [1:246022] 71.7 95.2 108.9 90.7 79.4 ...

 $ PneumoVaxEver            : chr [1:246022] "Yes" "Yes" "Yes" "Yes" ...

 $ HighRiskLastYear         : chr [1:246022] "No" "No" "No" "No" ...
 $ CovidPos                 : chr [1:246022] "No" "No" "Yes" "Yes" ...

 Length:246022      Length:246022     
 Class :character   Class :character  
 Mode  :character   Mode  :character

The dataset comes from the 2022 Behavioral Risk Factor Surveillance System (BRFSS) and includes 246,022 adults with 41 variables covering demographics, health behaviors, chronic conditions, and self-reported health status.

Variable Planning Notes

  • Outcome: HeartDisease (recoded from HadHeartAttack: Yes = 1, No = 0)
  • Primary exposure: HadDiabetes (Yes/No)
  • Key covariates to include:
    • AgeCategory (ordered factor)
    • Sex
    • BMI (continuous)
    • SmokerStatus (4 levels: Never smoked, Former smoker, Current every day, Current some days)
    • PhysicalActivities (Yes/No)
    • GeneralHealth (ordered: Excellent, Very good, Good, Fair, Poor)
    • RaceEthnicityCategory
  • RQ1: HadDiabetes is associated with HeartDisease, adjusted for AgeCategory, Sex, BMI, SmokerStatus
  • RQ2: PhysicalActivities is associated with HeartDisease, adjusted for BMI, AgeCategory
  • RQ3: SmokerStatus x AgeCategory interaction on HeartDisease
  • RQ4: GeneralHealth is associated with HeartDisease, adjusted for AgeCategory, Sex, BMI, SmokerStatus, HadDiabetes_binary, PhysicalActivities
  • Other variables to consider as covariates:
    • SleepHours
    • AlcoholDrinkers
    • HadDepressiveDisorder
    • DifficultyWalking
    • MentalHealthDays — covariate only, not a main RQ
# Recode heart attack status as a binary outcome
heart <- heart |>
  mutate(HeartDisease = if_else(HadHeartAttack == "Yes", 1, 0))

# Check that the recode did not create missing values
sum(is.na(heart$HeartDisease))
[1] 0
# Compare original and recoded values
table(heart$HadHeartAttack, heart$HeartDisease, useNA = "always")
      
            0      1   <NA>
  No   232587      0      0
  Yes       0  13435      0
  <NA>      0      0      0
# Count respondents by heart disease status and calculate percentages
# Count how many people are in each group
heart |>
  count(HeartDisease) |>

  # Add labels and percentages
  mutate(
    Label = if_else(HeartDisease == 1, "Heart Attack", "No Heart Attack"),
    Percent = round(n / sum(n) * 100, 1) # round to the nearest tenth
  ) |>

  # Keep only the columns we want to show
  select(Label, n, Percent) |>

  # Display as a clean formatted table
  knitr::kable(col.names = c("Heart Disease Status", "N", "Percent (%)"))
Heart Disease Status N Percent (%)
No Heart Attack 232587 94.5
Heart Attack 13435 5.5

The outcome variable, HeartDisease, was recoded from HadHeartAttack into a binary indicator (1 = heart attack, 0 = no heart attack). A cross-tabulation confirmed that no mismatches or missing values were introduced during the recoding process. Of the 246,022 respondents, 13,435 (5.5%) reported a history of heart attack, indicating that heart disease cases represent a relatively small proportion of the sample and should be considered when evaluating model performance.

Week 2: Factor Recoding and EDA

# Check unique values before recoding
unique(heart$HadDiabetes)
[1] "No"                                     
[2] "Yes"                                    
[3] "Yes, but only during pregnancy (female)"
[4] "No, pre-diabetes or borderline diabetes"
unique(heart$AgeCategory)
 [1] "Age 65 to 69"    "Age 70 to 74"    "Age 75 to 79"    "Age 80 or older"
 [5] "Age 50 to 54"    "Age 40 to 44"    "Age 60 to 64"    "Age 55 to 59"   
 [9] "Age 45 to 49"    "Age 35 to 39"    "Age 25 to 29"    "Age 30 to 34"   
[13] "Age 18 to 24"   
unique(heart$SmokerStatus)
[1] "Former smoker"                        
[2] "Never smoked"                         
[3] "Current smoker - now smokes every day"
[4] "Current smoker - now smokes some days"
unique(heart$GeneralHealth)
[1] "Very good" "Fair"      "Good"      "Excellent" "Poor"     
unique(heart$PhysicalActivities)
[1] "Yes" "No" 
unique(heart$Sex)
[1] "Female" "Male"  
unique(heart$RaceEthnicityCategory)
[1] "White only, Non-Hispanic"      "Black only, Non-Hispanic"     
[3] "Other race only, Non-Hispanic" "Multiracial, Non-Hispanic"    
[5] "Hispanic"                     
# Factor Recoding

# Recode HadDiabetes into binary yes/no
# Removing diabetes associated with pregnancy
# Pre-diabetes grouped as No (noted as a limitation)

heart <- heart|>
  mutate(
    HadDiabetes_binary = case_when(
      HadDiabetes == "Yes" ~ "Yes",
      HadDiabetes == "No" ~ "No",
      HadDiabetes == "No, pre-diabetes or borderline diabetes" ~ "No",
      HadDiabetes == "Yes, but only during pregnancy (female)" ~ NA_character_
    ),
    HadDiabetes_binary = factor(HadDiabetes_binary, levels = c("No", "Yes"))
  )

# Check how many gestational diabetes cases were excluded
table(heart$HadDiabetes, useNA = "always")

                                     No No, pre-diabetes or borderline diabetes 
                                 204834                                    5392 
                                    Yes Yes, but only during pregnancy (female) 
                                  33813                                    1983 
                                   <NA> 
                                      0 
table(heart$HadDiabetes_binary, useNA = "always")

    No    Yes   <NA> 
210226  33813   1983 
# Recode all remaining covariates as factors
heart<- heart |>
  mutate(
    # factor version of outcome for table display
    HeartDisease_f = factor(HeartDisease, levels = c(0, 1),
                            labels = c("No", "Yes")),
    # Self-reported health (Excellent = reference)
    GeneralHealth = factor(
      GeneralHealth,
      levels = c("Excellent", "Very good",
                 "Good", "Fair", "Poor")
    ),
    
    # Age groups (youngest group = reference)
    AgeCategory = factor(
      AgeCategory,
      levels = c("Age 18 to 24", "Age 25 to 29",
                 "Age 30 to 34", "Age 35 to 39",
                 "Age 40 to 44", "Age 45 to 49",
                 "Age 50 to 54", "Age 55 to 59",
                 "Age 60 to 64", "Age 65 to 69",
                 "Age 70 to 74", "Age 75 to 79",
                 "Age 80 or older")
    ),
    
    # Smoking history (never smoked = reference)
    SmokerStatus = factor(
      SmokerStatus,
      levels = c("Never smoked",
                 "Former smoker",
                 "Current smoker - now smokes every day",
                 "Current smoker - now smokes some days")
    ),
    
    # Binary covariates
    PhysicalActivities = factor(PhysicalActivities, levels = c("No", "Yes")),
    Sex = factor(Sex, levels = c("Female", "Male")),
    
    #Race/ethnicity (White only, Non-Hispanic" = reference)
    RaceEthnicityCategory = factor(
      RaceEthnicityCategory,
      levels = c("White only, Non-Hispanic",
                 "Black only, Non-Hispanic",
                 "Hispanic",
                 "Other race only, Non-Hispanic",
                 "Multiracial, Non-Hispanic")
    )
  )

# Quick check before modeling
levels(heart$HadDiabetes_binary)
[1] "No"  "Yes"
levels(heart$GeneralHealth)
[1] "Excellent" "Very good" "Good"      "Fair"      "Poor"     
levels(heart$AgeCategory)
 [1] "Age 18 to 24"    "Age 25 to 29"    "Age 30 to 34"    "Age 35 to 39"   
 [5] "Age 40 to 44"    "Age 45 to 49"    "Age 50 to 54"    "Age 55 to 59"   
 [9] "Age 60 to 64"    "Age 65 to 69"    "Age 70 to 74"    "Age 75 to 79"   
[13] "Age 80 or older"
levels(heart$SmokerStatus)
[1] "Never smoked"                         
[2] "Former smoker"                        
[3] "Current smoker - now smokes every day"
[4] "Current smoker - now smokes some days"
levels(heart$PhysicalActivities)
[1] "No"  "Yes"
levels(heart$Sex)
[1] "Female" "Male"  
levels(heart$RaceEthnicityCategory)   
[1] "White only, Non-Hispanic"      "Black only, Non-Hispanic"     
[3] "Hispanic"                      "Other race only, Non-Hispanic"
[5] "Multiracial, Non-Hispanic"    
# Create BMI categories using standard WHO cutoffs
heart <- heart |>
  mutate(
    BMICategory = case_when(
      BMI < 18.5 ~ "Underweight",
      BMI < 25 ~ "Normal",
      BMI < 30 ~ "Overweight",
      BMI >= 30 ~ "Obese"
    ),
    # Reference level = Normal
    BMICategory = factor(
      BMICategory,
      levels = c("Normal", "Underweight", "Overweight", "Obese")
    )
  )

# Check categories
table(heart$BMICategory)

     Normal Underweight  Overweight       Obese 
      70425        3755       87468       84374 
library(gtsummary) # for the tbl_summary()

# Create Overall population summary

# Keep only variables used in the summary
heart |>
  select(
    HeartDisease_f, HadDiabetes_binary, AgeCategory, Sex, BMI,
    BMICategory, SmokerStatus, PhysicalActivities, GeneralHealth,
    RaceEthnicityCategory, SleepHours, AlcoholDrinkers,
    HadDepressiveDisorder, DifficultyWalking
  ) |>

  # Create descriptive summary of variables
  tbl_summary(
    # rename variables for clearer display in output
    label = list(
      HeartDisease_f ~ "Heart Disease",
      HadDiabetes_binary ~ "Diabetes",
      AgeCategory ~ "Age Category",
      BMICategory ~ "BMI Category",
      SmokerStatus ~ "Smoking Status",
      PhysicalActivities ~ "Physical Activity",
      GeneralHealth ~ "General Health",
      RaceEthnicityCategory ~ "Race/Ethnicity",
      SleepHours ~ "Sleep Hours",
      AlcoholDrinkers ~ "Alcohol Use",
      HadDepressiveDisorder ~ "Depressive Disorder",
      DifficultyWalking ~ "Difficulty Walking"
    ),
    # Summarize continuous variables using mean and standard deviation
    # Summarize categorical variables using counts and percentages
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
    ),
    # Exclude missing values from the displayed summary
    missing = "no"
  ) |>
  bold_labels()

    White only, Non-Hispanic 186,336 (76%)

Difficulty Walking 36,070 (15%)
1 n (%); Mean (SD) 
# Create a table for stratified by HeartDisease status
# Divide overall population into smaller subgroups of yes/no for HeartDisease 

heart |>
  select(HeartDisease_f, HadDiabetes_binary, AgeCategory, Sex, BMI,
         BMICategory, SmokerStatus, PhysicalActivities, GeneralHealth,
         RaceEthnicityCategory, SleepHours, AlcoholDrinkers,
         HadDepressiveDisorder, DifficultyWalking) |>
  tbl_summary(
    by = HeartDisease_f,
    label = list(
      HadDiabetes_binary ~ "Diabetes",
      AgeCategory ~ "Age Category",
      BMICategory ~ "BMI Category",
      SmokerStatus ~ "Smoking Status",
      PhysicalActivities ~ "Physical Activity",
      GeneralHealth ~ "General Health",
      RaceEthnicityCategory ~ "Race/Ethnicity",
      SleepHours ~ "Sleep Hours",
      AlcoholDrinkers ~ "Alcohol Use",
      HadDepressiveDisorder ~ "Depressive Disorder",
      DifficultyWalking ~ "Difficulty Walking"
    ),
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
    ),
    # Round continuous variables to 1 decimal place
    digits = all_continuous() ~ 1,
    missing = "no"
  ) |>
  # Add overall sample column
  add_overall() |>
  # Add p-values for group comparisons
  add_p() |>
  bold_labels() |>
  bold_p(t = 0.05) # Shows p-values <= 0.05 in bold and shows which covariates
Characteristic Overall
N = 246,0221		 No
N = 232,5871		 Yes
N = 13,4351		 p-value2
Diabetes 33,813 (14%) 29,159 (13%) 4,654 (35%) <0.001

    Age 50 to 54 19,913 (8.1%) 19,210 (8.3%) 703 (5.2%)
    Age 55 to 59 22,224 (9.0%) 21,112 (9.1%) 1,112 (8.3%)
    Age 60 to 64 26,720 (11%) 25,145 (11%) 1,575 (12%)
    Age 65 to 69 28,557 (12%) 26,402 (11%) 2,155 (16%)
    Age 70 to 74 25,739 (10%) 23,331 (10%) 2,408 (18%)
    Age 75 to 79 18,136 (7.4%) 16,071 (6.9%) 2,065 (15%)
    Age 80 or older 17,816 (7.2%) 15,390 (6.6%) 2,426 (18%)
Sex


<0.001

<0.001

<0.001

<0.001

2 Pearson’s Chi-squared test; Wilcoxon rank sum test 
# are significant.
# t-tests for continuous variables
t.test(BMI ~ HeartDisease_f, data = heart)

    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  BMI by HeartDisease_f
t = -14.947, df = 14993, p-value < 2.2e-16
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group No and group Yes is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.9862529 -0.7575750
sample estimates:
 mean in group No mean in group Yes 
         28.62052          29.49244 
t.test(SleepHours ~ HeartDisease_f, data = heart)

    Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  SleepHours by HeartDisease_f
t = -1.4099, df = 14341, p-value = 0.1586
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means between group No and group Yes is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 -0.055029875  0.008985513
sample estimates:
 mean in group No mean in group Yes 
         7.020074          7.043096 
# Chi-square tests for categorical variables
chisq.test(table(heart$HadDiabetes_binary, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction

data:  table(heart$HadDiabetes_binary, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 5193.4, df = 1, p-value < 2.2e-16
chisq.test(table(heart$AgeCategory, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  table(heart$AgeCategory, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 7925.1, df = 12, p-value < 2.2e-16
chisq.test(table(heart$Sex, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction

data:  table(heart$Sex, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 1321.8, df = 1, p-value < 2.2e-16
chisq.test(table(heart$BMICategory, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  table(heart$BMICategory, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 355.74, df = 3, p-value < 2.2e-16
chisq.test(table(heart$SmokerStatus, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  table(heart$SmokerStatus, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 2241.3, df = 3, p-value < 2.2e-16
chisq.test(table(heart$PhysicalActivities, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction

data:  table(heart$PhysicalActivities, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 1701.6, df = 1, p-value < 2.2e-16
chisq.test(table(heart$GeneralHealth, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  table(heart$GeneralHealth, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 9893.9, df = 4, p-value < 2.2e-16
chisq.test(table(heart$RaceEthnicityCategory, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test

data:  table(heart$RaceEthnicityCategory, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 195.92, df = 4, p-value < 2.2e-16
chisq.test(table(heart$AlcoholDrinkers, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction

data:  table(heart$AlcoholDrinkers, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 1353.2, df = 1, p-value < 2.2e-16
chisq.test(table(heart$HadDepressiveDisorder, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction

data:  table(heart$HadDepressiveDisorder, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 138, df = 1, p-value < 2.2e-16
chisq.test(table(heart$DifficultyWalking, heart$HeartDisease_f))

    Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction

data:  table(heart$DifficultyWalking, heart$HeartDisease_f)
X-squared = 6286.6, df = 1, p-value < 2.2e-16

Univariable analyses indicated that all categorical covariates were significantly associated with heart disease status (all p < 0.001). The largest chi-square statistics were observed for general health (X² = 9893.9), age category (X² = 7925.1), and difficulty walking (X² = 6286.6), suggesting these variables have the strongest crude (not adjusted for other variables) associations with heart disease. Diabetes (X² = 5193.4), smoking status (X² = 2241.3), physical activity (X² = 1701.6), and alcohol use (X² = 1353.2) also showed strong associations, while race/ethnicity (X² = 195.9) and depressive disorder (X² = 138.0) had weaker but still significant associations. For the continuous covariates, mean BMI was significantly higher among respondents with heart disease (29.5 for yes vs. 28.6 for no; p < 0.001), whereas sleep hours did not differ meaningfully between groups (7.04 for yes vs. 7.02 for no; p = 0.16). These results support including the planned covariates in the multivariable model. Sleep hours showed a weaker univariable association and may instead be evaluated in sensitivity analyses. Notably, diabetes showed one of the strongest crude associations with heart disease (X² = 5193.4, p < 0.001), providing initial support for the primary hypothesis.

library(corrplot)

# Convert binary Yes/No variables to numeric for correlation converted to 1/0

cor_vars <- heart |>
  mutate(
    PhysicalActivities_num = if_else(PhysicalActivities == "Yes", 1, 0),
    AlcoholDrinkers_num = if_else(AlcoholDrinkers == "Yes", 1, 0),
    HadDepressiveDisorder_num = if_else(HadDepressiveDisorder == "Yes", 1, 0),
    DifficultyWalking_num = if_else(DifficultyWalking == "Yes", 1, 0),
    HadDiabetes_num = if_else(HadDiabetes_binary == "Yes", 1, 0),
    Sex_num = if_else(Sex == "Male", 1, 0)
  ) |>
  # Keep variables used in the correlation analysis
select(
  HeartDisease, BMI, SleepHours, PhysicalHealthDays, MentalHealthDays,
  # binary variables converted to 0/1
  PhysicalActivities_num, AlcoholDrinkers_num, HadDepressiveDisorder_num,
  DifficultyWalking_num, HadDiabetes_num, Sex_num
)

# Compute the correlation matrix
# Use complete observations only (rows with no missing values)
cor_matrix <- cor(cor_vars, use = "complete.obs")
cor_matrix

# Plot the correlation heatmap
corrplot(
  cor_matrix,
  method = "color",
  # Show as an upper triagular matrix
  type = "upper",

  # Smaller labels for variables
  tl.cex = 0.6,
  tl.col = "black",

  # Smaller correlation numbers
  number.cex = 0.5,
  addCoef.col = "black",
  
  # Rotate labels for more space
  tl.srt = 45,

  col = colorRampPalette(c("royalblue3", "white", "red3"))(200),
  title = "Correlation Matrix of Numeric and Binary Variables",
  # Increase margins slightly
  mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0)
)

The correlation matrix did not suggest strong multicollinearity among the covariates, as all pairwise correlations were below 0.45. Diabetes status showed moderate positive correlations with heart disease (r = 0.15) and BMI (r = 0.20), which is consistent with established relationships between diabetes, obesity, and cardiovascular disease. These findings further support including BMI as a potential confounder in the primary regression model. MentalHealthDays showed a very weak correlation with both heart disease (r = 0.03) and diabetes (r = 0.03), providing additional justification for excluding it from the primary model.

# Class imbalance documentation
# One outcome group (heart disease = yes/no -> 0/1) is much smaller than the other

# Calculate sample size and outcome prevalence
n_total <- nrow(heart)  # total number of observations in the dataset
n_total
[1] 246022
# Count number of positive (heart disease = 1) cases
n_positive <- sum(heart$HeartDisease == 1, na.rm = TRUE)
n_positive
[1] 13435
# Compute percentage of positive cases
percentage_positive <- round(n_positive / n_total * 100, 2)
percentage_positive
[1] 5.46

Of the 246,022 respondents, 13,435 (5.46%) reported a history of heart attack. Because respondents without heart disease greatly outnumber those with heart disease, the dataset shows substantial class imbalance. This is common in large population-based health datasets and is important to consider when evaluating model performance. For example, a model predicting no heart disease for every respondent would still achieve 94.54% accuracy despite having no ability to identify individuals with heart disease. For this reason, model performance will be evaluated primarily using ROC curve analysis and the area under the curve (AUC), rather than overall accuracy alone.

Week 3: Logistic Regression Modeling

# Fitting the univariate logistic regression model for each covariate
library(broom) # load broom package for tidying model outputs

# Covariates for univariate logistic regression
covariates <- c(
  "HadDiabetes_binary", "AgeCategory", "Sex", "BMI",
  "BMICategory", "SmokerStatus", "PhysicalActivities",
  "GeneralHealth", "RaceEthnicityCategory"
)

# Fit one logistic regression model for each covariate using lapply()
# var is each covariate being looped through one at a time
crude_models <- lapply(covariates, function(var) {
  # Create model formula: HeartDisease ~ covariate
  formula <- as.formula(paste("HeartDisease ~", var))
  
  # glm() to fit logistic regression model
  model <- glm(
    formula,
    data = heart,
    family = binomial
  )
  
  # Extract crude odds ratios, confidence intervals, and p-values
  tidy(model, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
    # Remove the intercept row
    filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
    # Add the covariate name to the results
    # This creates a new column called "covariate"
    mutate(covariate = var)
})

# Combine all model results into one data frame using bind_rows()
crude_results <- bind_rows(crude_models)

# Results table
crude_results |>
  # Select relevant columns
  select(covariate, term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  # Round values
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 2),
    conf.low = round(conf.low, 2),
    conf.high = round(conf.high, 2),
    p.value = ifelse(p.value <0.001, "<0.001", round(p.value, 3))
  ) |>
  # Display as a clean formatted table
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Covariate", "Term", "Crude OR",
                  "95% CI Lower", "95% CI Upper", "P-value"),
    caption = "Crude Odds Ratios from Univariable Logistic Regression"
  )
Crude Odds Ratios from Univariable Logistic Regression
Covariate Term Crude OR 95% CI Lower 95% CI Upper P-value
HadDiabetes_binary HadDiabetes_binaryYes 3.69 3.55 3.83 <0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 25 to 29 1.11 0.74 1.66 0.606
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 30 to 34 1.78 1.26 2.53 0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 35 to 39 2.64 1.93 3.67 <0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 40 to 44 3.56 2.64 4.89 <0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 45 to 49 6.72 5.06 9.13 <0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 50 to 54 9.57 7.26 12.92 <0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 55 to 59 13.77 10.48 18.53 <0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 60 to 64 16.38 12.49 22.00 <0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 65 to 69 21.34 16.30 28.64 <0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 70 to 74 26.98 20.62 36.20 <0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 75 to 79 33.59 25.65 45.10 <0.001
AgeCategory AgeCategoryAge 80 or older 41.21 31.49 55.30 <0.001
Sex SexMale 1.93 1.86 2.00 <0.001
BMI BMI 1.02 1.02 1.02 <0.001
BMICategory BMICategoryUnderweight 1.30 1.12 1.50 <0.001
BMICategory BMICategoryOverweight 1.37 1.31 1.44 <0.001
BMICategory BMICategoryObese 1.55 1.48 1.62 <0.001
SmokerStatus SmokerStatusFormer smoker 2.32 2.23 2.41 <0.001
SmokerStatus SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day 2.36 2.23 2.49 <0.001
SmokerStatus SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days 1.91 1.74 2.08 <0.001
PhysicalActivities PhysicalActivitiesYes 0.47 0.45 0.49 <0.001
GeneralHealth GeneralHealthVery good 2.01 1.84 2.21 <0.001
GeneralHealth GeneralHealthGood 4.35 4.00 4.75 <0.001
GeneralHealth GeneralHealthFair 9.60 8.80 10.49 <0.001
GeneralHealth GeneralHealthPoor 18.83 17.14 20.72 <0.001
RaceEthnicityCategory RaceEthnicityCategoryBlack only, Non-Hispanic 0.79 0.73 0.84 <0.001
RaceEthnicityCategory RaceEthnicityCategoryHispanic 0.65 0.60 0.69 <0.001
RaceEthnicityCategory RaceEthnicityCategoryOther race only, Non-Hispanic 0.83 0.76 0.90 <0.001
RaceEthnicityCategory RaceEthnicityCategoryMultiracial, Non-Hispanic 1.06 0.95 1.18 0.313

Univariable logistic regression showed that most covariates were significantly associated with heart disease, except for the Age 25 to 29 group (OR = 1.11, p = 0.606) and the Multiracial, Non-Hispanic group (OR = 1.06, p = 0.313). Diabetes had a strong association with heart disease (OR = 3.69, 95% CI: 3.55–3.83), with individuals reporting diabetes having nearly four times the odds compared to those without diabetes. There was a clear increasing trend in odds across age groups. General health followed an ordinal (ranking-wise) pattern consistent with expectations, and physical activity was associated with lower odds of heart disease (OR = 0.47). These crude estimates serve as the baseline for the multivariable model.

# Multivariable model for RQ1
# Is diabetes associated with higher odds of heart disease after 
# adjusting for age, sex, BMI, and smoking status?
model_rq1 <- glm(
  HeartDisease ~ HadDiabetes_binary + AgeCategory + 
    Sex + BMI + SmokerStatus,
  data = heart,
  family = binomial
)

# Extract adjusted OR's and 95% CI's
tidy(model_rq1, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  # Remove any intercepts
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 2),
    conf.low = round(conf.low, 2),
    conf.high = round(conf.high, 2),
    p.value = ifelse(p.value <0.001, "<0.001", round(p.value, 3))
  ) |>
  # Select results to display
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Term", "Adjusted OR",
                  "95% CI Lower", "95% CI Upper", "P-value"),
    caption = "Adjusted Odds Ratios from the Primary Multivariable Model"
  )
Adjusted Odds Ratios from the Primary Multivariable Model
Term Adjusted OR 95% CI Lower 95% CI Upper P-value
HadDiabetes_binaryYes 2.34 2.25 2.43 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 25 to 29 0.96 0.64 1.44 0.855
AgeCategoryAge 30 to 34 1.43 1.02 2.04 0.043
AgeCategoryAge 35 to 39 1.99 1.46 2.77 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 40 to 44 2.54 1.88 3.49 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 45 to 49 4.67 3.51 6.35 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 50 to 54 6.57 4.98 8.88 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 55 to 59 9.23 7.02 12.43 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 60 to 64 11.08 8.45 14.90 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 65 to 69 14.41 11.00 19.36 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 70 to 74 18.46 14.09 24.79 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 75 to 79 23.71 18.08 31.86 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 80 or older 33.40 25.49 44.87 <0.001
SexMale 2.00 1.93 2.08 <0.001
BMI 1.02 1.02 1.02 <0.001
SmokerStatusFormer smoker 1.62 1.56 1.69 <0.001
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day 2.72 2.57 2.88 <0.001
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days 2.49 2.26 2.73 <0.001 
# Compare crude vs adjusted OR for HadDiabetes_binary
crude_or <- crude_results |>
  filter(term == "HadDiabetes_binaryYes") |>
  select(estimate, conf.low, conf.high)

adjusted_or <- tidy(model_rq1, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term == "HadDiabetes_binaryYes") |>
  select(estimate, conf.low, conf.high)

# Display side by side
bind_rows(
  crude_or |> mutate(model = "Crude"),
  adjusted_or |> mutate(model = "Adjusted")
) |>
  # Round all numeric results to 2 decimal places
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), round, 2)) |>
  select(model, estimate, conf.low, conf.high) |>
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Model", "OR", "95% CI Lower", "95% CI Upper"),
    caption = "Crude vs. Adjusted OR for Diabetes and Heart Disease"
  )
Crude vs. Adjusted OR for Diabetes and Heart Disease
Model OR 95% CI Lower 95% CI Upper
Crude 3.69 3.55 3.83
Adjusted 2.34 2.25 2.43

After adjusting for age, sex, BMI, and smoking status, adults with diabetes had 2.34 times the odds of heart disease compared with non-diabetic adults (95% CI: 2.25–2.43, p < 0.001). This was lower than the crude odds ratio of 3.69, indicating that part of the unadjusted association was explained by differences in age, BMI, sex, and smoking status between the two groups. For example, individuals with diabetes tend to be older on average, and older adults are more likely to have heart disease regardless of diabetes status. However, diabetes remained a strong and statistically significant predictor of heart disease after adjustment, supporting the primary hypothesis.

Among other covariates, male sex (OR = 2.00), older age groups, and current daily smoking (OR = 2.72) were also independently associated with higher odds of heart disease. BMI showed a small but significant positive association (OR = 1.02 per unit increase). The Age 25 to 29 group remained non-significant after adjustment (OR = 0.96, p = 0.855).

# Multivariable model for RQ2
# Does physical inactivity independently predict heart disease after adjusting for BMI and age?

model_rq2 <- glm(
  HeartDisease ~ PhysicalActivities + BMI + AgeCategory,
  data = heart,
  family = binomial
)
tidy(model_rq2, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  # Remove any intercepts
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 2),
    conf.low = round(conf.low, 2),
    conf.high = round(conf.high, 2),
    p.value = ifelse(p.value <0.001, "<0.001", round(p.value, 3))
  ) |>
  # Select results to display
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Term", "Adjusted OR",
                  "95% CI Lower", "95% CI Upper", "P-value"),
    caption = "Adjusted Odds Ratios for Physical Activity Model"
  )
Adjusted Odds Ratios for Physical Activity Model
Term Adjusted OR 95% CI Lower 95% CI Upper P-value
PhysicalActivitiesYes 0.60 0.58 0.62 <0.001
BMI 1.02 1.02 1.03 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 25 to 29 1.06 0.71 1.57 0.79
AgeCategoryAge 30 to 34 1.63 1.16 2.33 0.005
AgeCategoryAge 35 to 39 2.40 1.76 3.34 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 40 to 44 3.17 2.36 4.36 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 45 to 49 5.90 4.44 8.01 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 50 to 54 8.29 6.28 11.19 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 55 to 59 11.92 9.07 16.04 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 60 to 64 14.42 11.00 19.37 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 65 to 69 19.13 14.61 25.68 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 70 to 74 24.27 18.54 32.56 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 75 to 79 30.27 23.12 40.65 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 80 or older 37.07 28.32 49.75 <0.001

After adjusting for BMI and age, physically active adults had 0.60 times the odds of heart disease compared with inactive adults (95% CI: 0.58–0.62, p < 0.001), indicating that physical activity was associated with lower odds of heart disease. BMI remained a small but statistically significant predictor (OR = 1.02 per unit increase). The odds of heart disease also continued to increase across older age groups, which was consistent with expectations. These findings suggest that physical inactivity is independently associated with heart disease even after accounting for BMI and age.

# Interaction model for RQ3
# Is the association between smoking and heart disease modified by age group?
model_rq3 <- glm(
  HeartDisease ~ SmokerStatus * AgeCategory + BMI + Sex,
  data = heart,
  family= binomial
)

# Interaction terms only
tidy(model_rq3, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  # keep only interaction terms containing :
  filter(str_detect(term, ":")) |>
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 2),
    conf.low = round(conf.low, 2),
    conf.high = round(conf.high, 2),
    p.value = ifelse(p.value < 0.001, "<0.001", round(p.value, 3))
  ) |>
  # select results to display
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Term", "Adjusted OR", 
                  "95% CI Lower", "95% CI Upper", "P-value"),
    caption = "Adjusted Odds Ratios for Smoking × Age Interaction Terms"
  )
Adjusted Odds Ratios for Smoking × Age Interaction Terms
Term Adjusted OR 95% CI Lower 95% CI Upper P-value
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 25 to 29 0.36 0.10 1.20 0.095
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 25 to 29 0.29 0.09 0.87 0.028
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 25 to 29 0.64 0.11 3.73 0.607
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 30 to 34 0.56 0.21 1.60 0.252
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 30 to 34 0.56 0.23 1.43 0.206
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 30 to 34 0.88 0.21 4.45 0.864
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 35 to 39 0.70 0.29 1.87 0.439
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 35 to 39 0.54 0.24 1.32 0.153
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 35 to 39 1.03 0.30 4.81 0.962
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 40 to 44 0.73 0.32 1.90 0.485
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 40 to 44 0.39 0.18 0.94 0.027
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 40 to 44 0.90 0.27 4.12 0.876
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 45 to 49 0.75 0.34 1.92 0.516
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 45 to 49 0.40 0.19 0.94 0.025
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 45 to 49 1.61 0.53 6.99 0.451
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 50 to 54 0.66 0.30 1.67 0.339
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 50 to 54 0.40 0.19 0.92 0.021
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 50 to 54 1.69 0.57 7.24 0.401
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 55 to 59 0.76 0.35 1.90 0.52
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 55 to 59 0.41 0.20 0.93 0.022
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 55 to 59 1.42 0.49 6.06 0.571
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 60 to 64 0.81 0.37 2.02 0.616
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 60 to 64 0.36 0.17 0.81 0.008
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 60 to 64 1.06 0.36 4.50 0.925
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 65 to 69 0.69 0.32 1.71 0.372
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 65 to 69 0.34 0.17 0.78 0.006
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 65 to 69 1.33 0.46 5.64 0.642
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 70 to 74 0.59 0.27 1.46 0.207
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 70 to 74 0.28 0.14 0.64 0.001
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 70 to 74 0.97 0.34 4.14 0.967
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 75 to 79 0.55 0.26 1.38 0.162
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 75 to 79 0.21 0.10 0.49 <0.001
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 75 to 79 0.74 0.25 3.18 0.627
SmokerStatusFormer smoker:AgeCategoryAge 80 or older 0.49 0.23 1.22 0.09
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day:AgeCategoryAge 80 or older 0.18 0.08 0.42 <0.001
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days:AgeCategoryAge 80 or older 0.50 0.16 2.22 0.289

After adjusting for BMI and sex, there was evidence that the association between smoking and heart disease differed across age groups. For current daily smokers, the interaction terms were statistically significant in older age categories, including Age 70–74 (OR = 0.28, 95% CI: 0.14–0.64, p = 0.001), Age 75–79 (OR = 0.21, 95% CI: 0.10–0.49, p < 0.001), and Age 80 or older (OR = 0.18, 95% CI: 0.08–0.42, p < 0.001). These interaction ORs below 1 indicate that the combined effect of daily smoking and older age on heart disease is smaller than would be expected based on their main effects alone, suggesting the smoking-heart disease association attenuates in the oldest age groups.

These results suggest that the relationship between smoking status and heart disease is not the same across age groups. For current daily smokers, the association with heart disease becomes weaker in older age groups, with lower odds ratios observed in older adults compared with younger adults. For former and occasional smokers, the estimates do not show a consistent pattern across age categories and are not consistently statistically significant. This provides evidence that age modifies the association between smoking and heart disease after accounting for BMI and sex.

# Multivariable model for RQ4
# Is general health status associated with heart disease after adjusting for behavioral and clinical factors?
model_rq4 <- glm(
  HeartDisease ~ GeneralHealth + AgeCategory + Sex + BMI +
    SmokerStatus + HadDiabetes_binary + PhysicalActivities,
  data = heart,
  family = binomial
)

tidy(model_rq4, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  # Remove any intercepts
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
  mutate(
    estimate = round(estimate, 2),
    conf.low = round(conf.low, 2),
    conf.high = round(conf.high, 2),
    p.value = ifelse(p.value <0.001, "<0.001", round(p.value, 3))
  ) |>
  # Select results to display
  select(term, estimate, conf.low, conf.high, p.value) |>
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Term", "Adjusted OR",
                  "95% CI Lower", "95% CI Upper", "P-value"),
    caption = "Adjusted Odds Ratios for General Health Model"
  )
Adjusted Odds Ratios for General Health Model
Term Adjusted OR 95% CI Lower 95% CI Upper P-value
GeneralHealthVery good 1.67 1.52 1.83 <0.001
GeneralHealthGood 3.04 2.78 3.33 <0.001
GeneralHealthFair 5.66 5.17 6.22 <0.001
GeneralHealthPoor 9.69 8.76 10.73 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 25 to 29 1.00 0.67 1.50 0.984
AgeCategoryAge 30 to 34 1.51 1.07 2.15 0.021
AgeCategoryAge 35 to 39 2.14 1.56 2.98 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 40 to 44 2.66 1.97 3.66 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 45 to 49 4.73 3.56 6.44 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 50 to 54 6.40 4.85 8.65 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 55 to 59 8.60 6.54 11.59 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 60 to 64 10.31 7.85 13.87 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 65 to 69 13.81 10.54 18.56 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 70 to 74 17.25 13.16 23.18 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 75 to 79 21.64 16.50 29.10 <0.001
AgeCategoryAge 80 or older 28.12 21.45 37.79 <0.001
SexMale 2.11 2.03 2.19 <0.001
BMI 1.00 1.00 1.01 0.001
SmokerStatusFormer smoker 1.45 1.39 1.51 <0.001
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day 1.93 1.82 2.05 <0.001
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days 1.84 1.67 2.03 <0.001
HadDiabetes_binaryYes 1.72 1.65 1.79 <0.001
PhysicalActivitiesYes 0.91 0.87 0.95 <0.001

After adjusting for age, sex, BMI, smoking, diabetes, and physical activity, general health status remained strongly associated with heart disease. Compared with adults reporting excellent health, the odds of heart disease increased steadily across each health category: 1.67 for very good health, 3.04 for good health, 5.66 for fair health, and 9.69 for poor health (all p < 0.001). This overall pattern remained after adjustment, indicating that worse self-reported health was consistently associated with higher odds of heart disease. The crude odds ratio for poor health was 18.83, which decreased to 9.69 after adjustment, suggesting that part of the original association was explained by differences in age, smoking, diabetes, and physical activity across health status groups.

library(car) # Needed for VIF

# VIF for primary model (RQ1)
# Check for multicollinearity among covariates
vif(model_rq1)
                       GVIF Df GVIF^(1/(2*Df))
HadDiabetes_binary 1.083548  1        1.040936
AgeCategory        1.151056 12        1.005879
Sex                1.014007  1        1.006979
BMI                1.124512  1        1.060430
SmokerStatus       1.084804  3        1.013659

Generalized variance inflation factors (GVIFs) were calculated for all covariates in the primary model. All adjusted GVIF values were below 1.07, which is well below the usual cutoff of concern which is 5, suggesting that multicollinearity was not a problem in the model. This indicates that the covariates were not highly correlated with one another and could be included together in the regression analysis.

Week 4: Logistic Regression Model Evaluation and Fit Assessment

# Full model with all covariates including optional ones
model_full <- glm(
  HeartDisease ~ HadDiabetes_binary + AgeCategory + Sex + BMI +
    SmokerStatus + PhysicalActivities + GeneralHealth +
    RaceEthnicityCategory + SleepHours + AlcoholDrinkers +
    HadDepressiveDisorder + DifficultyWalking,
  data = heart,
  family = binomial
)

# Compare AIC between the primary and full model
AIC(model_rq1, model_full)
           df      AIC
model_rq1  19 89873.42
model_full 32 85173.24

The AIC for the full model is 85173.24 which is lower than the AIC for the primary model which is 89873.42. Since lower AIC values indicate a better balance between model fit and model complexity, the full model provided a better fit for the data. This suggests that the additional covariates provided useful information for predicting heart disease in the model.

However, AIC was used as a secondary measure rather than the primary criterion for model selection. The primary model was chosen based on the research question and findings from previous studies, with diabetes as the main exposure and adjustment for age, sex, BMI, and smoking status as key confounding variables. Although the full model produced a lower AIC, it includes several additional predictors that were not central to the primary research question. The full model will be examined further when evaluating overall model performance in Week 5, while the primary model remains the main model used to address RQ1.

# Install required package for the test
library(ResourceSelection)

# Hosmer-Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test for primary model
# g = 10 is the standrad number of groups used in the test

# Filter out NAs before fitting
heart_complete <- heart |> filter(!is.na(HadDiabetes_binary))

# Refit primary model on complete cases
model_rq1 <- glm(
  HeartDisease ~ HadDiabetes_binary + AgeCategory + Sex + BMI +
    SmokerStatus,
  data = heart_complete,
  family = binomial
)

# Hypotheses:
# H0: The model fits the data adequately
# (no significant difference between the observed and predicted values)

# HA: The model does not fit the data adequately

hoslem.test(
  heart_complete$HeartDisease,
  fitted(model_rq1),
  g = 10
)

    Hosmer and Lemeshow goodness of fit (GOF) test

data:  heart_complete$HeartDisease, fitted(model_rq1)
X-squared = 99.412, df = 8, p-value < 2.2e-16

The Hosmer-Lemeshow test was significant (χ² = 99.41, df = 8, p < 0.001), suggesting that the model does not fit the data perfectly. However, the sample size was very large (n = 244,039), and with datasets of this size, even small differences between observed and predicted values can produce statistically significant results. As a result, the Hosmer-Lemeshow test may indicate lack of fit even when the model performs reasonably well in practice.

Model performance will again be assessed using ROC/AUC in Week 5, which is a better measure of how well the model distinguishes between cases and non-cases of heart disease.

# Residual plots for primary model

# Add fitted values and residuals to a data frame
# Run diagnostics for the primary model
model_rq1_diag <- data.frame(
  fitted = fitted(model_rq1),
  deviance_resid = residuals(model_rq1, type = "deviance"),
  pearson_resid = residuals(model_rq1, type = "pearson")
)

# Visualizations of deviance residuals vs fitted values

# Fitted values are the predicted probabilities
ggplot(model_rq1_diag, aes(x = fitted, y = deviance_resid)) +
  # Adjust transparency and size of point
  geom_point(alpha= 0.1, size = 1) +
  # Add horizontal line
  # Place the reference line at 0
  geom_hline( yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "royalblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Deviance residuals vs Fitted Values",
    x = "Fitted Values",
    y = "Deviance Residuals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()
ggplot(model_rq1_diag, aes(x = fitted, y = pearson_resid)) +
  geom_point(alpha= 0.1, size = 1) +
  geom_hline( yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "purple") +
  labs(
    title = "Pearson residuals vs Fitted Values",
    x = "Fitted Values",
    y = "Pearson Residuals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

The Deviance residuals vs Fitted Values plot showed two distinct bands above and below zero, which is expected for a logistic regression with a binary outcome. Most observations had very low predicted probabilities of heart disease, reflecting the low prevalence of the outcome in the sample. At these low fitted values, the upper band included several points with deviance residuals above 3, representing cases where the model assigned a low probability of heart disease but the outcome was present. Both the upper (HeartDisease = 1) and lower bands (HeartDisease = 0) showed a slight downward curvature, suggesting that the magnitude of residuals decreased as predicted probabilities increased, with relatively better fit for observations assigned higher probabilities of heart disease. As fitted values increased toward 0.5, the points became more scattered.

The Pearson residuals vs Fitted Values plot showed a similar two-band structure above and below zero, consistent with a binary outcome. Most observations again had very low predicted probabilities of heart disease. The upper band displayed a more noticeable downward curvature compared with the deviance residual plot, while the lower band remained closer to zero across the range of fitted values. Pearson residuals reached values as high as 25, reflecting cases where the model predicted a very low probability of heart disease but the outcome occurred. As fitted values increased toward 0.5, the points became more dispersed without a clear pattern. Overall, both plots showed patterns consistent with what is expected for a logistic regression model with a binary outcome and class imbalance.

# Influence Measures

# Cook's distance and hat values for primary model
# Standard cutoff for influential observations = 4/n

n <- nrow(heart_complete)
cutoff <- 4 / n
cutoff
[1] 1.639082e-05
# Compute influence measures in dataframe
influence_measures <- data.frame(
  cooks = cooks.distance(model_rq1),
  hat = hatvalues(model_rq1)
)

# Number of observations above Cook's distance cutoff
n_cooks <- sum(influence_measures$cooks > cutoff)
n_cooks 
[1] 13471
# Percentage above Cook's distance cutoff
cooks_percent <- 100 * n_cooks / n
cooks_percent
[1] 5.520019
# Cook's Distance Plot
ggplot(
  influence_measures,
  aes(x = seq_along(cooks), y = cooks)
) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.3) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = cutoff,
    linetype = "solid",
    color = "red"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Cook's Distance for Primary Model",
    x = "Observation Index",
    y = "Cook's Distance"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Cook’s distance shows that most observations are clustered very close to zero, indicating that the majority do not meaningfully affect the model. A total of 13,471 observations (5.52%) exceeded the standard cutoff of 4/n (1.64 \times 10^{-5}). The plot shows several horizontal tiers of points above the cutoff, reflecting groups of observations that share similar predictor combinations and therefore exert a similar, relatively uniform level of influence on the fitted model. A smaller number of points sits well above these bands, indicating observations with stronger individual influence whose removal would produce more noticeable changes in the model estimates compared to the majority of the data.

# Hat values (leverage)
summary(influence_measures$hat)
     Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max. 
2.202e-05 4.037e-05 5.975e-05 7.786e-05 9.324e-05 2.681e-03 
# Hat value cutoff = 2p/n (rule of thumb for high leverage)
# p = number of model parameters (including intercept)
# n = sample size

hat_cutoff <- 2 * length(coef(model_rq1)) / n
hat_cutoff
[1] 0.0001557128
# Number of observations above leverage cutoff
n_hat <- sum(influence_measures$hat > hat_cutoff)
n_hat
[1] 21713
# Percentage above leverage cutoff
hat_percent <- 100 * n_hat / n
hat_percent
[1] 8.897348
# Hat value plot
ggplot(influence_measures, aes(x = seq_along(hat), y = hat)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2, size = 0.3) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = hat_cutoff,
    linetype = "solid",
    color = "red"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Hat Values for Primary Model",
    x = "Observation Index",
    y = "Hat Value"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Hat values show that most observations are clustered very close to zero, indicating low leverage and typical predictor values. A total of 21,713 observations (8.90%) exceeded the cutoff of 2p/n (0.0001557128). Above the cutoff, points become more scattered, reflecting a gradual increase in leverage for observations with less common predictor combinations. A small number of points sit further above the main cluster, indicating relatively higher leverage and greater potential influence on the model if paired with large residuals, though all hat values remain small in absolute terms.

# Remove observations above Cook's distance cutoff
heart_no_influence <- heart_complete[influence_measures$cooks <= cutoff, ]

# Refit model
model_rq1_no_influence <- glm(
  HeartDisease ~ HadDiabetes_binary + AgeCategory +
    Sex + BMI + SmokerStatus,
  data = heart_no_influence,
  family = binomial
)

summary(model_rq1_no_influence)

Call:
glm(formula = HeartDisease ~ HadDiabetes_binary + AgeCategory + 
    Sex + BMI + SmokerStatus, family = binomial, data = heart_no_influence)

Coefficients:
                                                    Estimate Std. Error z value
(Intercept)                                       -2.657e+01  4.483e+03  -0.006
HadDiabetes_binaryYes                             -9.459e-13  2.331e+03   0.000
AgeCategoryAge 25 to 29                           -1.793e-13  4.627e+03   0.000
AgeCategoryAge 30 to 34                           -3.081e-13  4.436e+03   0.000
AgeCategoryAge 35 to 39                           -3.724e-13  4.291e+03   0.000
AgeCategoryAge 40 to 44                           -4.387e-13  4.225e+03   0.000
AgeCategoryAge 45 to 49                           -3.741e-13  4.243e+03   0.000
AgeCategoryAge 50 to 54                           -3.249e-13  4.101e+03   0.000
AgeCategoryAge 55 to 59                           -3.296e-13  4.028e+03   0.000
AgeCategoryAge 60 to 64                           -4.190e-13  3.907e+03   0.000
AgeCategoryAge 65 to 69                            4.292e-12  3.878e+03   0.000

AgeCategoryAge 75 to 79                           -6.658e-13  4.276e+03   0.000

SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days  9.324e-14  4.248e+03   0.000
                                                  Pr(>|z|)
(Intercept)                                          0.995
HadDiabetes_binaryYes                                1.000
AgeCategoryAge 25 to 29                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 30 to 34                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 35 to 39                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 40 to 44                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 45 to 49                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 50 to 54                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 55 to 59                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 60 to 64                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 65 to 69                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 70 to 74                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 75 to 79                              1.000
AgeCategoryAge 80 or older                           1.000
SexMale                                              1.000
BMI                                                  1.000
SmokerStatusFormer smoker                            1.000
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes every day    1.000
SmokerStatusCurrent smoker - now smokes some days    1.000

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 0.0000e+00  on 230567  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 1.3377e-06  on 230549  degrees of freedom
AIC: 38

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 25
# Compare odds ratios
exp(coef(model_rq1_no_influence))
                                      (Intercept) 
                                     2.900701e-12 
                            HadDiabetes_binaryYes
